title: Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction as a coherence diagnostic for chirality-induced spin selectivity
abstract: Whether chirality-induced spin selectivity (CISS) reflects coherent SU(2) spin rotation or incoherent spin-dependent filtering is a central unresolved question in molecular spintronics, with implications ranging from asymmetric chemistry to quantum information. We show that these two scenarios are distinguishable by a sharp symmetry criterion on the superexchange interaction mediated by a chiral molecular bridge. Coherent CISS, implemented as a unitary spin rotation of the tunneling electron, generates a giant Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction with ratio |D|/JH up to 3, which is two orders of magnitude beyond intrinsic Rashba spin-orbit coupling in Si/SiGe. Incoherent CISS, represented by any Hermitian (non-unitary but spin-diagonal) tunneling matrix, produces D = 0 identically; we prove this as a structural theorem, reinforced by a Lindblad argument that dissipative spin filtering cannot modify virtual-tunneling-mediated superexchange. The DM interaction thus serves as a coherence order parameter, nonzero only when quantum amplitudes for opposite-spin transmission maintain a fixed relative phase. We derive closed-form angular, enantiomeric, and sensitivity signatures and show that the critical coherent rotation angle lies two orders of magnitude below current transport-inferred values and is accessible to existing 10 kHz exchange spectroscopy in gate-defined quantum dots. Five candidate molecules are predicted to exceed this threshold by one to two orders of magnitude even in a conservative interface-amplification scenario. The proposed measurement converts a long-standing transport controversy into a binary spin-qubit experiment with quantum-amplitude resolution.
summary: This paper proposes using the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction as a diagnostic tool to determine if chiral molecules induce spin selectivity through coherent rotation or incoherent filtering. It demonstrates that a non-zero DM interaction would uniquely certify a coherent quantum process. The study shows that this measurement is achievable with current quantum dot technologies using several candidate molecules.
main_problem: Resolving the long-standing controversy of whether Chirality-Induced Spin Selectivity (CISS) is a coherent SU(2) spin rotation or an incoherent spin-dependent filtering process.
main_result: The paper proves that the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interaction serves as a definitive coherence order parameter, being non-zero only in the coherent rotation scenario and identically zero in the incoherent filtering scenario.
method: The author uses Schrieffer-Wolff projection to derive an effective spin Hamiltonian and employs Lindblad analysis to show that dephasing does not generate DM interaction.
model_or_system: The system consists of two electrons in adjacent gate-defined quantum dots coupled via virtual tunneling through a chiral molecular bridge, modeled either as a unitary spin rotator or a non-unitary spin-diagonal barrier.
key_observables: Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (D), singlet-triplet gap (delta E_ST), exchange anisotropy ratio (|D|/JH), and spin polarization (P).
important_parameters: Rotation angle (theta_C), helix axis (n), exchange energy (J0), and the 10 kHz exchange spectroscopy threshold.
main_assumptions: The virtual tunneling time is much shorter than the molecular dephasing time; the tunneling matrix in the incoherent model is assumed to be Hermitian.
figures_summary: Figure 3 illustrates the three experimental signatures: the angular dependence of the gap, the enantiomer sign-reversal test, and the sensitivity threshold for detecting rotation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CISS controversy, defines two competing physical models, derives a symmetry theorem for the DM interaction, proposes an experimental protocol using quantum dots, and evaluates the feasibility for specific candidate molecules.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental 'no-go' theorem for spin-dependent transport and proposes a way to use spin-qubit exchange spectroscopy to probe the quantum coherence of molecular interfaces.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two spin qubits QD1 and QD2 coupled through a chiral molecular bridge. The helix axis ˆn subtends angle ϕhelix with the magnetic field B. Coherent CISS (Model 1) enacts a unitary SU(2) rotation on the tunneling electron. Incoherent CISS (Model 2) acts as a non-unitary diagonal spin filter. The two scenarios give opposite predictions for the superexchange tensor Eq. (4).

FIG. 3. Three falsifiable experimental signatures. (a) An- gular fingerprint: singlet-triplet gap ∆EST versus helix-field angle ϕhelix for four values of θC under Model 1; gray dotted line: Model 2 (any η) and interface Rashba SOC, both angle- independent. All Model 1 curves emanate from the gauge- trivial value J0 = 5 µeV at ϕhelix = 0 and deviate as sin2 ϕhelix. (b) Enantiomer null test: Model 1 predicts Dx(R) = −Dx(S) with JH invariant; Model 2 shows no enantiomer dependence. (c) Sensitivity threshold: |∆ECISS ST | versus θC for ˆn ⊥B, with 10 kHz, 100 kHz and 1 MHz precision thresholds marked. Detection at θC ∼0.03π (i.e., P ∼0.2%) is within reach of current Si/SiGe exchange...

FIG. 2. The two CISS models give qualitatively different su- perexchange tensors. (a) Model 1: irreducible components JH, |D|, |←→ Γ | versus θC (ˆn ⊥B); analytic Eqs. (5)–(7) (black dashed) agree with numerical evaluation of Eq. (4) to machine precision. (b) Model 2 components versus filtering asymmetry η: |D| = 0 identically. (c) Full 3 × 3 exchange tensor at matched transport polarization P = 25%: Model 1 has off-diagonal antisymmetric entries (the DM contribution), Model 2 is strictly diagonal. (d) Anisotropy ratio |D|/JH ver- sus P: the two models are separated by 2–3 decades, with the experimental Ge FinFET value [33] indicated for reference. Parameters: t0 = 50 µeV, U = 2 meV, J0 =...
